steps.title: Identify the key distinction in the passage
steps.content:
The passage says NH₃ on rocky planets can only reach detectable levels through biological activity, but on mini-Neptunes it forms naturally from atmospheric conditions. This planet-type distinction determines whether NH₃ is a reliable biosignature.
  • Choice A — Its presence is more likely to indicate that a planet is a mini-Neptune than that the planet is a rocky planet that could support life.: The passage is about whether NH₃ indicates life, not about what type of planet a body is. This reverses the logic. ✗
  • Choice B — Its absence from a planet that's not a mini-Neptune indicates that the planet probably doesn't have life.: The passage says NH₃ presence on rocky planets signals life; it says nothing about absence implying no life. ✗
  • Choice C — It should be treated as a biosignature gas if detected in the atmosphere of a rocky planet but not if detected in the atmosphere of a mini-Neptune.: This directly captures the passage's finding: NH₃ signals life on rocky planets (where biological activity is needed to produce detectable amounts) but not on mini-Neptunes (where it forms naturally). ✓
  • Choice D — It doesn't reliably reach high enough concentrations in the atmospheres of rocky planets or mini-Neptunes to be treated as a biosignature gas.: Contradicts the passage, which says NH₃ can reach detectable levels on rocky planets through biological activity. ✗

steps.title: Apply the distinction to confirm the answer
steps.content: The researchers found NH₃ behaves differently depending on planet type: biosignature on rocky planets, not on mini-Neptunes. The correct answer captures this conditional logic without reversing or contradicting the passage. That confirms C is correct.
